title: A Unified SU(2) Framework for Vector Beam Transformations and Complex Beam Shaping
abstract: We present a constructive framework for designing transformations between structured light fields using birefringent optical elements, formulated in terms of SU(2) operations on polarization. Within this framework, transformations between vector beams are treated as spatially varying SU(2) operations, leading to a direct procedure for designing doubly inhomogeneous waveplates (d-plates) that implement the desired mapping. We identify a condition under which a single element implements a prescribed transformation exactly, including the global phase, and provide an explicit prescription for constructing the corresponding doubly inhomogeneous waveplate (d-plate) when this condition is satisfied, along with its realization using a finite sequence of singly inhomogeneous plates, including a QHQ configuration. Within this formulation, a broad class of problems in structured light can be treated within a single framework, including vector beam transformations, spin-orbital dynamics, and complex beam shaping. Crucially, the same SU(2) operations directly realize quantum channels on the orbital angular momentum degree of freedom, with polarization serving as a physical ancilla. These results establish a unified and explicitly constructive route to complex beam shaping and vector beam transformations based on SU(2) parameter synthesis, and provide a systematic foundation for designing next-generation photonic elements for structured light and spin-orbit information processing.
summary: This paper introduces a unified SU(2) mathematical framework for designing optical elements that can precisely transform the polarization and phase of light. It demonstrates that these elements can be used to create complex structured light beams and can even simulate multi-step quantum walks in a single optical step.
main_problem: Designing a unified framework for controlling both phase and polarization degrees of freedom to implement complex beam shaping and vector beam transformations.
main_result: The authors establish a constructive SU(2) framework that allows for the design of doubly-inhomogeneous waveplates (d-plates) to implement arbitrary transformations, including the realization of instantaneous quantum walks on the orbital angular momentum degree of freedom.
method: The study uses SU(2) group theory and Pauli operators to formulate spatially varying transformations, deriving closed-form solutions for waveplate retardance and orientation.
model_or_system: The physical platform consists of birefringent optical elements, specifically d-plates and q-plates, acting on light fields with both polarization (spin) and orbital angular momentum (OAM) degrees of freedom.
key_observables: State of Polarization (SoP), Orbital Angular Momentum (OAM) spectrum, Stokes vectors, and complex-electric field distributions.
important_parameters: Retardance (Gamma), fast-axis orientation (alpha), rotation axis (k), and topological charge (m).
main_assumptions: The transformations are assumed to be unitary (no intensity loss), and the rotation axis for standard waveplates is restricted to the X-Y plane.
figures_summary: Figures show polar plots of SU(2) parameters, the construction of skyrmion Full Poincare Beams, and the generation of specific angle states and OAM distributions.
paper_structure: The paper begins by defining the SU(2) mathematical framework for polarization and OAM, progresses to the constructive design of d-plates and waveplate sequences, demonstrates applications in complex beam shaping and Full Poincare Beams, and concludes by linking these operations to quantum walk dynamics.
why_interesting: It provides a direct mapping between classical structured light manipulation and quantum information processing, specifically showing how polarization can act as an ancilla to implement quantum channels and instantaneous quantum walks on the OAM degree of freedom.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

The plot towards the left hand side represents the parameters of SU(2) plate with orange line indicating the rotation angle Γ, the red and green lines indicating θ and φ respectively, of the rotation axis. The plots towards the right hand side represents the param- eters of ˆH ˆQ1 ˆQ2, the three waveplate equivalent of the SU(2) plate. Here, the orange line represents retardance Γ and blue line represents fast-axis orientation α.

FIG. 2: (a), (b) Transformations corresponding to ˆS1 and ˆS2 respectively, given in eqn. (38). The plots to- wards the left hand side represent the parameters of the SU(2) plate with orange line indicating the rotation an- gle Γ, the red and green lines indicating θ and φ respec- tively, of the rotation axis. The plots towards the right hand side represent the parameters of ˆH ˆQ1 ˆQ2, the three waveplate equivalent of the SU(2) plate with orange line representing retardance Γ and blue line representing fast axis orientation α.

FIG. 3: The d-plate and waveplate parameters corre- sponding to the transformation between a scalar SoP and an FPB. The plot towards left hand side shows the d- plate parameters and the plots towards right hand side shows the two waveplate equivalent of the same. (a) Transformation from a scalar beam with horizontal SoP to skyrmion FPB with right-circular polarization state at the center, (b) Transformation from skyrmion FPB with right-circular polarization at the center to a scalar beam with horizontal SoP. The plots describes the fast axis orientation and the retardance at each point of the plate where, retardance is represented as a color coded background.

FIG. 4: (a-e) The d-plates required for generating the five angle states |2, j⟩, for j = −2, . . . , 2 respectively, where the orange line indicates retardance Γ and the blue line indicates fast-axis orientation α.

FIG. 5: (a-e) The d-plates required for generating the five OAM states |oi⟩, for i = 1, . . . , 5 of eqn. (54) respectively, where the orange line indicates retardance Γ and the blue line indicates fast-axis orientation α.
